name: A GFCI clears every ground fault in the house
statement: When a hot conductor faults to a metal enclosure, the GFCI in the panel senses the imbalance and opens the circuit — that's how the fault is cleared.
correction: GFCIs detect imbalance between hot and neutral (at 4–6 mA) and only protect specific branch circuits required by code — kitchens, bathrooms, laundry, outdoors, and similar. Most circuits in a house rely on the overcurrent breaker to clear a ground fault. The breaker only trips because the main bonding jumper turns a hot-to-case fault into a high-current short circuit through the EGC and service neutral back to the utility transformer. Without that bond, even a GFCI-protected circuit would leave any unprotected appliance frame energized; the bond is what makes fast, predictable clearing possible system-wide.
